speaker: interviewee
text: I was talking about the little boy. He was full of snow, and I picked him up. And I didn't even know he's dead. And I don't know how I survived then. If I didn't get a stroke or something, I don't know. I still don't believe it, that I survived just holding him in my arms-- dead. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I missed something. When I left the [NON-ENGLISH], and the Salzmans helped me, they took me to their-- to a-- to their-- to a place. His name was Gentle, but he was really in nature a gentle person. It was very cold-- I was almost frozen to death. And he picked me up, and he took me on-- on his attic-- his attic. And he-- he put sheep skin-- he covered me with sheep skin and I was there for three days. And then I started to recover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was our tragedy. We didn't have partisans. We didn't even know that they exist. This was a big tragedy. That's why they managed to kill all the few thousands in this forest til the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other people who helped that you want to mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not helped, but we were together. We were running together. One is in Winnipeg, one is in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names, could you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- one night before we we were liberated, we didn't know that the Russians had already so close. So they tried to-- the Ukrainian tried to kill as many Jews as the could. So they killed my best girlfriend with her mother. They killed the last 200. And we were running. This was January the 4th, '44. And we were running. We didn't know what direction. And my girlfriend, the one who lives in Winnipeg, they left her mother frozen, and this was a terrible thing to live through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to leave her on the snow because she was frozen, and we were running. All of us couldn't die because of her. So this-- so my friend, til today, she's very sick. She's in and out of hospitals.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaya Legoorvitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's in Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- because we knew by the killings that they're-- they're scared, the Ukrainians-- they didn't want witnesses. So they made sure about the killings, the last night, that the Russians must be very close. So they ran from the place, it calls Kudrinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we ran. And we came to a place, Stara Huta. Can you imagine? We came there and the Russians already. The Russian soldiers were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Polacks still were so horrible. They took out two young boys and they told the Russian soldiers that those boys helped with the Germans and they took them out and they killed them. This was already when the Russian occupied our-- But I got very sick on typhus. And the people had to leave me, because I was on the floor with Polish people. And everybody was still, you know, trying to get somewhere, not in one place. So I was with typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very sick. In the Stara Huta. Very sick. And I was on the floor. And there was no medicine, because people were dying from-- there were new cemeteries from the typhus. But two Polish ladies, they didn't mean any harm, but they didn't want I should suffer. So at that time they used to think if you have fever, if you put a blanket on you, you get-- the fever disappeared, but this wasn't the case. It's not the case. So they took me out. They wanted I shouldn't suffer-- I should die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me out. They took me out in a terrible, terrible storm, and they took me back to the little house and put me on the floor. And they were sure that in the morning I'll be dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before they took me, they took off the little earrings-- that I have the picture still with the earrings that my mother gave me. They didn't want to bury me with the golden earrings. They took this I remember, because I couldn't hear, I couldn't move, but I could see. And in the morning, they walked in. They wanted to bury me. And I looked at them, and they got very scared. They started like to-- you know they were really scared to see me alive. And I survived. No matter-- no matter the worst circumstances, I survived. And if people ask me how, I don't know, because I can't believe it myself-- that I could sit still and talk to you about it. It's very hard to believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you were actually liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Kudrinka. Kudrinka. Not Kudrinka, Stara Huta. Stara Huta. We ran from Kudrinka to Stara Huta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't tell you still-- I forgot to tell you-- that I have a friend that I was with in kindergarten. Her name was Yetta Fingerhut. She ran away. She ran away with her mother. She was an only child, only girl. The mother and family, aunties, a few aunties and cousins.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one policeman shot all of them, but the bullet went through her neck, and it stuck in one place that you could survive. And we find her. She survived and the bullet was removed after in Israel. This was really-- like-- talk about miracles. And where were you liberated? When you were liberated, where were you liberated to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stara Huta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the liberators there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't do nothing with me. I was free. I could be on the floor, in-- any place. So I was with Polacks still. A Jew, Mr. Edelman, he took me to their family and I was like adopted in their family. They still-- they came to Canada to my son's bar mitzvah. You know. And they were very, very good. They-- they were very extremely, very good to me. And they lost three-- three daughters in the war. They survived with two sons. One is in Montreal now and one is in-- they're like brothers to me. I don't-- I call them my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Tuchyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- for one day. I wish I wouldn't go. I came to Tuchyn and I didn't even recognize our place. Everything was destroyed. It was almost horrible. I was so hysterical that Russian soldiers cried seeing me in what position I was. It was a terrible experience. Some of us remained there for a couple-- for a little while longer, but I couldn't. I felt being there, I'll die. I just want to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through one-- you get-- people tell you. The Ukrainian told you-- the Polacks. My mother and brother, and other six people, eight of them, they were very cold, so they went in to get warm a little. So they took them out, and they shoot those eight people. And they shot my brother first, my mother should watch. And then they told me about it. And my mother was 36 years old when she was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find anything left in your house when you came there? Was the house still standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was destroyed. Everything. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There was just a part-- I don't know-- we didn't have refrigerators, so I don't know how you call it in English, that in the ground, you could keep milk, and butter. So my Zayde, we had one-- and only one side of it remained. But nothing, everything was burned, everything was destroyed. It was the most horrible. I don't think I could ever, til I'll forget the day I came for one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the war wasn't over, it was still on. It was '44. We were liberated but the war was over at '45. So we were afraid to go to bigger cities because of the bombardments. The Germans used to bombard terrible in the evenings. So we still stayed in the villages. So I stayed in the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day I decided I have to move, because some Jewish people they already moved to cities. So I decided I want to go too, so I-- with two more orphans. It was-- how do you travel? You travel on trains with the soldiers, because it was a war. You didn't need a-- a ticket. Just if you got place. So from this village I went a place, Rokitno, with those two orphans. And then I wanted to go to Rowne. So coming to Rokitno there was a stop. And I went into a Jewish family, that I never met, and I asked who could help me to get to Rowne. There was a young girl, her name was Zerka-- Gluss. This was now my sister-in-law. And she told me her brother came from Rowne for a day and is going back, and he'll help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he helped me with the two orphans to come to Rowne. And I came to Rowne, I went to one Polish lady that I knew. The-- the street I remember, [NON-ENGLISH] 5. [NON-ENGLISH] 5. He took me there and he went to work. Around 4 or 5 o'clock, I don't know exactly, he came back to the place. I was standing in the window, and he-- and he talked to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I don't want you should stay with Polacks. I have a bed, and a-- with a family. I'll give you the bed-- a bed. Can you imagine the bed? And I go-- and I go to the office. He felt very bad for me. So he took me to this family. There was one room. There was a mother and father, [NON-ENGLISH] is the name was, with a little boy. And my husband had a little-- I don't know what it was even. But as long inside, not outside. So in the next day-- and he was a partisan, my husband survived in the partisans-- so he got food from a kitchen. So in the morning, he came with food. And I didn't have stockings. I was barefoot. So he went, there was not far a market, and he bought for me a pair of lilac stockings.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they was the most beautiful stockings for me. [LAUGHS] And already-- and we remain together. He was very good to me. He was the most wonderful human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Rowne, when the war was over in '45, May the 9th, they decided to run. We wanted to go to Israel. So they told us, they'll go with you. We'll get to Italy, we'll get to Romania. We'll be able to go Israel, they'll help us. So we tried to run from Rowne, and we came to Poland. Katowice. And from there, there was already a Jewish organization, that they sent us as Greeks to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Do you know the name of the Jewish organization then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was from the-- I think it was from the Haganah. I think from the Israel Haganah. There was already kibbutzim there, because there were so many displaced people, displaced children. So the Jewish people organized. From Israel they send Haganah and they helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Czechoslovakia as Greeks. And from Czechoslovakia to Bratislava, into Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a nightmare because there were the barracks from the-- from the prisoners of war, and they weren't buried deep. So the smell was-- and I was already pregnant. I-- I wanted a baby. I wanted something on my own. I didn't realize that a baby needs food, and a baby needs diapers. My mind didn't work too well. I was too young even. Because I needed a mother myself, and I wanted to be a mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was pregnant. In all my pregnancy, I never slept in a bed, only on dirty floors because we were running. We wanted to board us, all kinds of boarders. And so I was barefoot, hungry, and pregnant. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So in the barracks was very, very hard. So again, there was the Jewish organization. And they tried, a pregnant woman, to send to Italy. In Italy there were a few kibbutzim. So they packed us in trains, and nobody should know there are people in the trains. They were-- they paid them off, you know? And in the middle of traveling-- my brother, who lives here, my brother lives with my husband's brother, David-- and we were-- we were closed in very dark trains, cabins. And one lady was having a baby all of a sudden. Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had to open the-- you know, this-- the-- the door, and they took everybody out, and they send them back. But we were 12, and we were at that time smarter. We run away in a different direction. And we went on a train and we came to Italy in the middle of the night. There was a place, they call it Della Samedia, where the [NON-ENGLISH] worked. We came in the middle of the night, and we got to the place. And we were on the sidewalks-- we were so tired we were lying on the sidewalks waiting for the daytime to let us in. And then they gave us a place in a kibbutz that a rich Jew had a villa, and he gave this for the Jewish people. At that time, I was very sick. After the war, I really fell apart. I was very strong in the war, but I fell apart. And there was a Jewish professor, his name was Madiano-- he was from Greece. And he took care of the refugees. And he took very good care of me, didn't want me to die. And he was extremely-- he really helped me to survive because I had trouble with my lungs. I had trouble holding my pregnancy, angiitis. And I had trouble with a breast. And I was operated five days of my ninth month. And it didn't even guarantee, not for me, not for the child. Thank God, I survived. And I had a little boy, Michael. And I was three years in Italy. The Italian people were the first people who were nice. They were so nice to us, to share the piece of bread with us. I could never forget them. For three years we stayed there. And my uncle from Canada, he sent papers through the [NON-ENGLISH]-- they knew where I was. This was my father's brother. He remembered me when I was four years old when he left Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac Stollar. He died. And he got an apartment for me on the [NON-ENGLISH], 127 [NON-ENGLISH]. And this will the nicest day for me in Canada, the most fortunate day. I met a family, I met first time another little girl. There was a knock on the door and a little girl of eight years. I was 21. She was eight. She came in. Her name was Reizela. And she told me, her mother sent her because she realized that-- I didn't even see her mother but her mother saw me-- that I need help. And since then, she became like my daughter. Til today she has a special place in my heart. She's married to a very nice-- to a very nice man with a nice family. But she's my best, closest friends. We can share good times and bad times. I know that I can always turn to Reizela.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other help besides Rezela's family when you came to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were very proud. My husband's a very proud person. He didn't even go to for help. He just went to-- he knew the--a little bit Italian. He went on Saint Lawrence, and he met the Jewish people. He went to a storekeeper, Basel Textile. And Basel Textile realized that my husband is an honest face. He gave him towels, and some, you know, dry goods. You should go try Italian community. And he became a peddler. But my husband was a very capable person. But I forgot to tell you that he was a presser before he became a peddler. He didn't know anything about pressing, but he had a wife and a little boy. And we didn't even have a spoon to feed the baby. And proud, he went to a factory, and he told them that he can press. And they paid him $18 a week. You couldn't live for $18 a week. So when he asked for a raise, they were very upset with him. So he left. And at that time, he went to the Saint Lawrence. He met those people, very nice and very helpful. And they trusted him. Without a penny, they gave him dry goods. And he made customers and the customers loved him. He was a honest, and a very-- my husband was a very special person. Everybody loved him. They always say that this man didn't have an enemy in his-- enemy in his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in what year that you came to Canada? Came to Montreal?

speaker: interviewer
text: In '48. Now tell us about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: After my first pregnancy I was scared of pregnancies. I had very bad time with the first one. Running, sleeping on the floors. I didn't even know how to sleep on a pillow. So I was very scared of pregnancies. But my son started to complain that he doesn't have a sister, doesn't have a brother. So I realized I'm selfish. And I have another little boy that he was-- that apple of our eye, Gary, after eight years. He was very special. He was a good scholar. He was already in the Talmud Torah. He was a very good scholar, very capable. But after my-- I lost, first, my husband to cancer 18 years ago. And then, eight years ago, I lost my son. And I have one son in Vancouver. where I get the strength, I don't know. Maybe-- maybe the love I received, I can still love and not to hate. I don't know what hate means. To me, charity is very important, to help. I always helped. That's one thing I made up my mind when I came here. When I'll have two pennies, I'll share the two pennies. And that's what I'm doing all my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they always say, for me, giving is a way of life. And to me-- and to me, duty is joy. I have a duty to others. I belong to a Hadassah-- I'm a very active member of Hadassah [NON-ENGLISH]. And I enjoy the work because I'm helping. Anything I can help, it makes me very, very happy. Now, tell us about your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two grandchildren in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Justine and Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have a granddaughter in Montreal. That she keep-- she's my sunshine, and she keeps me going. She's 13 years old now. That's where it's left after my son, when I lost him. She's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the girls in England, how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The girls in England. How old are they? How old are your granddaughters in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names and their age, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. One is 29, one is 27-- Rachel. And Justine is the older one. And Rachel is the younger one, 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you wanted to tell us your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to tell you the story never, never to forget the 6 million who perished in the most humiliating, inhuman way. And this should be never, never repeated again. That's why I'm telling the story. But this is not the whole story. It's only a drop on the ocean. That's not really what I went through. I feel that I'm deprived by not telling the whole story because it's like three books putting into pages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you can think of now that you wanted to say that you didn't have a chance to say while we were having this conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are so many things that I have to say. When I think of all of my friends, they didn't have a chance what I have. I have a chance to be still alive and to be in a country like Canada, to be free. So it's-- so it's very, very hard to think of those who perished in such a terrible way. When I go to a restaurant, I can never leave food because I feel guilt. I-- I went through so much-- I was so hungry that I prayed just to be able to be eat a loaf of bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suffered terrible. My suffering, there was-- you can't even describe it. Even when I want to, you can't describe the suffering. This is-- I'm telling you in a few minutes, and this was years.

speaker: interviewee
text: But thank God, I'm not-- you know, I-- I'm still very soft. I became softer after the war than before. That's what I can't understand. What makes me softer now? When I hear a cat cry, I feel sorry for them. This is the person who buried dead people and didn't even cry. Where do you think-- I'm, sorry. I was just going to ask you where you think you get this strength from? Where does it all come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think from my mother. Because you get so much, it stays with you. She gave me-- she didn't give me material thing, but she gave me so much love that I have love for everybody. I love people. I think my hobby-- when people ask me my hobby, I say people. I love people-- good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you very much for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to thank you because you made it-- you made it easier for me being you-- being what you are. And I hope we'll remain friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure we will.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it took very-- it's finished, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it--
